title: In-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (Code Blue) in a Monitored 58-Year-Old: High-Quality CPR, Rhythm-Based ACLS (Defibrillate SHOCKABLE VF/Pulseless VT — NOT Asystole/PEA), Epinephrine + Antiarrhythmic Timing, the H's and T's, and HIGH-PERFORMANCE CODE-TEAM LEADERSHIP with Role Assignment and CLOSED-LOOP Communication
subtitle: An IN-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST ('CODE BLUE') is the abrupt loss of effective cardiac function — the patient is UNRESPONSIVE, NOT breathing (or only gasping/agonal), and PULSELESS. RECOGNITION + BLS FIRST: confirm unresponsiveness, CALL FOR HELP / activate the code and call for the crash cart + DEFIBRILLATOR, and check breathing and a central (carotid) pulse SIMULTANEOUSLY for NO MORE THAN 10 SECONDS; if no definite pulse, begin HIGH-QUALITY CHEST COMPRESSIONS immediately. HIGH-QUALITY CPR is the foundation of survival: a RATE of 100-120/min, a DEPTH of at least 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than ~2.4 inches (6 cm) in adults, ALLOW FULL CHEST RECOIL between compressions, MINIMIZE INTERRUPTIONS (keep any pause under 10 seconds), ROTATE COMPRESSORS every 2 minutes (or sooner if fatigued) to maintain quality, use a firm surface/backboard, and AVOID EXCESSIVE VENTILATION; the compression-to-ventilation ratio is 30:2 until an advanced airway is placed, after which compressions are continuous with one breath every 6 seconds (~10/min). Apply defibrillator pads as soon as the device arrives and analyze the rhythm. ACLS is RHYTHM-BASED: SHOCKABLE rhythms — VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION (VF) and PULSELESS VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA (pVT) — are treated with IMMEDIATE DEFIBRILLATION (early defibrillation is the single most important determinant of survival in these rhythms), after which CPR is resumed IMMEDIATELY for 2 minutes before the next rhythm/pulse check; NON-SHOCKABLE rhythms — ASYSTOLE and PULSELESS ELECTRICAL ACTIVITY (PEA) — are NOT defibrillated and are treated with high-quality CPR, epinephrine, and correction of the underlying cause (a classic, dangerous error is attempting to 'shock asystole'). MEDICATIONS: EPINEPHRINE 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes (given as soon as possible in non-shockable arrest; after the second shock in shockable arrest); an ANTIARRHYTHMIC — AMIODARONE 300 mg (then 150 mg) or LIDOCAINE — is added for VF/pVT that is REFRACTORY to defibrillation. Obtain IV or IO access, consider an advanced airway, and attach WAVEFORM CAPNOGRAPHY (a sudden rise in ETCO2 suggests Return Of Spontaneous Circulation/ROSC; a persistently low ETCO2 <10 mmHg suggests poor compression quality). Pulse/rhythm checks occur about every 2 minutes, coinciding with the compressor switch, with interruptions minimized. The team must continually search for and treat REVERSIBLE CAUSES — the 'H's and T's': HYPOVOLEMIA, HYPOXIA, HYDROGEN ION (acidosis), HYPO-/HYPERKALEMIA, HYPOTHERMIA; TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX, cardiac TAMPONADE, TOXINS, and THROMBOSIS (pulmonary or coronary). CODE STATUS is verified — full ACLS is not provided to a patient with a valid DNR order. The chief NURSING-LEADERSHIP focus: a resuscitation runs best as a HIGH-PERFORMANCE TEAM with a clearly defined TEAM LEADER who maintains the overview, assigns ROLES, monitors CPR quality and the algorithm, makes decisions, and does NOT get tunnel-visioned doing a single task; defined ROLES include the compressor(s) (rotating every 2 minutes), airway/ventilation (BVM/respiratory therapy), the defibrillator operator, the IV/IO + medication nurse, the recorder/timekeeper, a runner, and a family liaison. Communication uses CLOSED-LOOP technique: the leader gives a CLEAR order to a SPECIFIC person ('Sara, give 1 mg epinephrine IV now'), the receiver REPEATS it back ('Giving 1 mg epinephrine IV now'), and CONFIRMS completion ('Epinephrine 1 mg IV given'); team members share information, speak up (constructive intervention), and the leader summarizes/reevaluates. The FIRST nurse at the bedside LEADS until the code team/provider arrives — starting CPR, calling the code, getting the defibrillator, and delegating. DELEGATION BY SCOPE: unlicensed assistive personnel can perform compressions and retrieve equipment and act as runners, while the RN performs assessment, defibrillation, and medication administration. On ROSC, the team transitions to POST-CARDIAC-ARREST CARE (optimize oxygenation/ventilation avoiding hyperoxia and hypocapnia, support blood pressure, obtain a 12-lead ECG and pursue PCI for STEMI, initiate targeted temperature management if comatose, and identify/treat the cause) and conducts a DEBRIEF. NURSING ROLE: recognize arrest and start high-quality CPR; defibrillate shockable rhythms early; deliver ACLS medications on schedule while minimizing interruptions; lead or function within a high-performance team using role assignment and closed-loop communication; identify and treat reversible causes; verify code status; and transition to post-arrest care.

patient.name: Frank D.
patient.code: FULL CODE (verified — no DNR); goal = high-quality CPR, early defibrillation of the shockable rhythm, rhythm-based ACLS, treat reversible causes, and run a coordinated high-performance code team to achieve and sustain ROSC
patient.admitDate: A 58-year-old man admitted to the cardiac step-down/telemetry unit with an acute coronary syndrome (rule-out MI) is being monitored when he suddenly clutches his chest, slumps, and becomes unresponsive. The bedside nurse finds him not breathing (only occasional agonal gasps) and pulseless within 10 seconds; the telemetry monitor shows VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION. A CODE BLUE is called, high-quality CPR is begun, and the crash cart/defibrillator is brought to the bedside. The resuscitation requires high-quality CPR, early defibrillation of the shockable rhythm, ACLS medications, a search for reversible causes (H's and T's), and coordinated high-performance team leadership with role assignment and closed-loop communication.
patient.room: Cardiac step-down/telemetry unit; witnessed, monitored arrest; crash cart + defibrillator at bedside; code team responding (code-team leader/provider, compressors, airway/RT, medication nurse, recorder, runner); transition to ICU and post-cardiac-arrest care after ROSC

vitals.hr: Ventricular fibrillation on monitor — no organized/perfusing rhythm
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; UNRESPONSIVE, not breathing, and PULSELESS within a 10-second check — CARDIAC ARREST; monitor shows VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION (a SHOCKABLE rhythm); call code blue, start CPR, get the defibrillator

vitals.hr: Compressions at 100-120/min generating a perfusing waveform
vitals.rr: BVM 30:2 (then 1 breath q6s after advanced airway); avoid hyperventilation
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; HIGH-QUALITY CPR: rate 100-120/min, depth ≥2 in (≤2.4 in), FULL recoil, interruptions <10 s, ROTATE compressors every 2 min, firm surface; waveform CAPNOGRAPHY attached (ETCO2 gauges compression quality; <10 mmHg = poor quality)

37.0°C; SHOCKABLE rhythm (VF/pVT) → DEFIBRILLATE immediately ('clear' team, ensure no one touching), then RESUME CPR IMMEDIATELY for 2 minutes before the next rhythm/pulse check — minimizing the peri-shock pause

37.0°C; EPINEPHRINE 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min; AMIODARONE 300 mg (then 150 mg) for refractory VF/pVT; team searches for and treats REVERSIBLE CAUSES — the H's and T's (hypovolemia, hypoxia, H+/acidosis, hypo-/hyperkalemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis)

vitals.hr: 98, sinus tachycardia (organized rhythm with a pulse)
vitals.temp: 36.8°C; RETURN OF SPONTANEOUS CIRCULATION — a palpable pulse and organized rhythm return (sudden ETCO2 rise heralded ROSC); post-arrest hypotension supported; begin post-cardiac-arrest care

labs.n: PRESENTATION + arrest recognition + high-quality CPR (BLS foundation)
labs.v: Frank D., a 58-year-old on telemetry for an acute coronary syndrome, suddenly becomes UNRESPONSIVE with agonal gasping; the bedside nurse confirms no breathing and NO PULSE within a ≤10-second check while the monitor shows VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION — an IN-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST. The immediate sequence: ACTIVATE the code (call for help / code blue) and call for the crash cart and DEFIBRILLATOR, and START HIGH-QUALITY CHEST COMPRESSIONS without delay. HIGH-QUALITY CPR is the foundation of survival and the nurse's first hands-on priority: compress at a RATE of 100-120/min, to a DEPTH of at least 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than ~2.4 inches (6 cm), ALLOW FULL RECOIL between compressions, MINIMIZE INTERRUPTIONS (any pause <10 seconds), ROTATE COMPRESSORS every 2 minutes to prevent fatigue and preserve quality, place the patient on a firm surface/backboard, and AVOID EXCESSIVE VENTILATION. Ventilate 30:2 until an advanced airway is placed, then give continuous compressions with one breath every 6 seconds (~10/min). Apply defibrillator pads as soon as the device arrives and analyze the rhythm. A witnessed, MONITORED arrest with prompt CPR and EARLY DEFIBRILLATION carries the best chance of survival, so seconds matter and the response must be organized.
labs.r: Cardiac arrest = unresponsive + not breathing (or agonal) + pulseless (check ≤10 s). Sequence: call code + get defibrillator + START high-quality CPR. HIGH-QUALITY CPR: rate 100-120/min, depth ≥2 in (≤2.4 in), FULL recoil, interruptions <10 s, ROTATE compressors q2min, firm surface, avoid over-ventilation; 30:2 until advanced airway then 1 breath q6s. Apply pads + analyze rhythm. RN role: recognize arrest + start high-quality CPR immediately.

labs.n: Rhythm-based ACLS — SHOCKABLE (VF/pVT) vs NON-SHOCKABLE (asystole/PEA) + medications
labs.v: ACLS is RHYTHM-BASED. SHOCKABLE rhythms are VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION (VF) and PULSELESS VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA (pVT): treat with IMMEDIATE DEFIBRILLATION — early defibrillation is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT determinant of survival in these rhythms — then RESUME CPR IMMEDIATELY for 2 minutes (do not pause to recheck the pulse right after a shock) before the next rhythm/pulse check, keeping the peri-shock pause minimal. NON-SHOCKABLE rhythms are ASYSTOLE and PULSELESS ELECTRICAL ACTIVITY (PEA): these are NOT defibrillated (a dangerous, classic error is to 'shock asystole') — treat with high-quality CPR, epinephrine, and correction of the underlying cause. MEDICATIONS: EPINEPHRINE 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes — given AS SOON AS POSSIBLE in a non-shockable arrest, and after the SECOND shock in a shockable arrest; an ANTIARRHYTHMIC is added for VF/pVT that is REFRACTORY to defibrillation — AMIODARONE 300 mg IV/IO (with a repeat 150 mg) or LIDOCAINE. Defibrillation 'clears' the team (no one touching the patient/bed) before the shock for safety. The cycle repeats: 2 minutes of CPR → rhythm/pulse check (at the compressor switch) → shock if shockable / no shock if non-shockable → medications on schedule → reassess, all while minimizing interruptions.
labs.r: SHOCKABLE = VF + pulseless VT → DEFIBRILLATE immediately (early defib = key to survival), resume CPR x2 min, then recheck. NON-SHOCKABLE = asystole + PEA → do NOT shock; CPR + epinephrine + fix the cause. EPINEPHRINE 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min (ASAP in non-shockable; after 2nd shock in shockable). AMIODARONE 300 then 150 mg (or lidocaine) for REFRACTORY VF/pVT. 'Clear' before shocking. RN role: identify shockable vs non-shockable + treat correctly.

labs.n: Reversible causes (H's and T's) + access, airway, and capnography
labs.v: Throughout the code the team must continually search for and TREAT REVERSIBLE CAUSES — the 'H's and T's' — because a code will not resolve until the underlying cause is corrected: HYPOVOLEMIA (give fluids/blood), HYPOXIA (oxygenate/ventilate/secure the airway), HYDROGEN ION/acidosis, HYPOKALEMIA or HYPERKALEMIA (e.g., calcium, insulin/dextrose, bicarbonate for hyperkalemia; replace for hypokalemia), HYPOTHERMIA (rewarm); TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX (needle/finger decompression), cardiac TAMPONADE (pericardiocentesis), TOXINS (antidotes — e.g., naloxone, bicarbonate for sodium-channel-blocker toxicity), and THROMBOSIS — pulmonary (massive PE) or coronary (acute MI). SUPPORT measures: obtain IV or IO access (IO if IV access is not rapidly achievable), consider an advanced airway (without prolonged interruption of compressions), and attach WAVEFORM CAPNOGRAPHY — a SUDDEN RISE in ETCO2 suggests ROSC, while a persistently LOW ETCO2 (<10 mmHg) signals INADEQUATE compression quality (push harder/faster, switch compressors). Point-of-care labs (potassium, glucose, ABG) and bedside ultrasound help identify causes. The team leader keeps the H's and T's on the verbal checklist and assigns someone to address likely causes (here, coronary thrombosis/acute MI is the probable trigger).
labs.r: Always hunt + treat REVERSIBLE CAUSES (H's & T's): Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion/acidosis, Hypo-/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (PE/MI). Support: IV/IO access, advanced airway (no long CPR pause), waveform CAPNOGRAPHY (sudden ETCO2 rise = ROSC; <10 = poor compressions). POC labs/ultrasound find causes. RN role: identify + help correct the cause while sustaining CPR.

labs.n: HIGH-PERFORMANCE CODE-TEAM LEADERSHIP — roles, closed-loop communication, delegation
labs.v: A resuscitation succeeds or fails on TEAM PERFORMANCE. There must be ONE clearly defined TEAM LEADER who maintains the OVERVIEW — directing the algorithm, ASSIGNING ROLES, monitoring CPR quality (rate/depth/recoil/interruptions), making decisions, and keeping the big picture — and who does NOT become 'tunnel-visioned' performing a single hands-on task. Defined ROLES distribute the work: COMPRESSOR(S) rotating every 2 minutes; AIRWAY/VENTILATION (BVM, respiratory therapy); the DEFIBRILLATOR operator; the IV/IO + MEDICATION nurse; the RECORDER/TIMEKEEPER (documents times of compressions, shocks, medications, and rhythm and prompts the 2-minute cycle and epinephrine intervals); a RUNNER (for supplies/labs/blood); and a FAMILY LIAISON (supporting family, who may be offered the option of presence with support). COMMUNICATION uses CLOSED-LOOP technique to prevent errors: the leader gives a CLEAR, DIRECTED order to a SPECIFIC person ('Sara, give 1 mg epinephrine IV now'), the receiver REPEATS it back ('Giving 1 mg epinephrine IV now'), and CONFIRMS completion ('Epinephrine 1 mg IV given, time 14:06'); team members share information openly, use CONSTRUCTIVE INTERVENTION (speak up if something is wrong or missed), and the leader periodically SUMMARIZES and REEVALUATES. The FIRST nurse at the bedside LEADS until the code team/provider arrives — calling the code, starting CPR, getting the defibrillator, and delegating. DELEGATION BY SCOPE: UNLICENSED ASSISTIVE PERSONNEL can perform CHEST COMPRESSIONS, retrieve the crash cart/equipment, and act as a runner; the RN performs ASSESSMENT, DEFIBRILLATION, and MEDICATION administration and other licensed tasks. After the event, a DEBRIEF reviews what went well and what to improve.
labs.r: High-performance team: ONE clear LEADER keeps the overview, assigns ROLES, monitors quality, decides — does NOT tunnel-vision on a task. Roles: compressors (rotate q2min), airway/RT, defib operator, med nurse, RECORDER/timer, runner, family liaison. CLOSED-LOOP communication: directed order to a named person → repeat-back → confirmation. First nurse LEADS until code team arrives. DELEGATE by scope: UAP = compressions/equipment/runner; RN = assessment/defib/meds. Debrief after. RN role: lead or function in a coordinated team.

labs.n: ROSC + post-cardiac-arrest care; code status; Pertinent NEGATIVES + debrief
labs.v: RETURN OF SPONTANEOUS CIRCULATION (ROSC) is recognized by a palpable pulse with an organized rhythm and often a SUDDEN RISE in ETCO2. Care then shifts to POST-CARDIAC-ARREST management (the start of which links to dedicated post-arrest TTM care): OPTIMIZE OXYGENATION/VENTILATION — titrate FiO2 to avoid HYPEROXIA (target SpO2 ~92-98%) and ventilation to avoid HYPOCAPNIA (avoid over-ventilation); SUPPORT BLOOD PRESSURE (fluids/vasopressors for post-arrest hypotension, target adequate MAP); obtain a 12-LEAD ECG and pursue emergent CORONARY ANGIOGRAPHY/PCI if STEMI or suspected coronary cause; initiate TARGETED TEMPERATURE MANAGEMENT if the patient remains comatose; identify and treat the precipitating cause; and transfer to ICU with a clear HANDOFF. CODE STATUS: full ACLS is provided only to a FULL CODE — a valid DNR order must be honored, so the team verifies code status. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the team did NOT delay compressions/defibrillation to obtain access or document first; did NOT 'shock' asystole/PEA; did NOT pause compressions for prolonged periods or hyperventilate the patient; did NOT run a leaderless, uncoordinated code with un-acknowledged orders; and did NOT continue full ACLS against a valid DNR. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: a witnessed, MONITORED arrest with immediate high-quality CPR and EARLY defibrillation of a shockable rhythm; a coordinated high-performance team with closed-loop communication; prompt epinephrine/antiarrhythmic and correction of the cause; and organized post-arrest care — all maximize survival with good neurologic outcome. A DEBRIEF after the code supports the team and improves future performance. NURSING ROLE: recognize arrest and start high-quality CPR; defibrillate shockable rhythms early; deliver ACLS medications and treat reversible causes; lead or function within a high-performance team using role assignment and closed-loop communication; verify code status; and transition to post-cardiac-arrest care.
labs.r: ROSC = pulse + organized rhythm (sudden ETCO2 rise). Post-arrest care: titrate O2 (avoid HYPEROXIA, SpO2 ~92-98%) + avoid hypocapnia; support BP; 12-lead + PCI for STEMI; TTM if comatose; treat cause; ICU handoff. Verify CODE STATUS (honor DNR). NEGATIVES (correct practice): no delaying CPR/defib, no shocking asystole/PEA, no long CPR pauses/hyperventilation, no leaderless code, no ACLS against valid DNR. RN role: ROSC recognition + post-arrest care + team leadership.

nursesNote: Cardiac step-down/telemetry — Frank D., 58, admitted for ACS (rule-out MI). WITNESSED, MONITORED arrest: sudden unresponsiveness + agonal gasps, NO pulse on ≤10-second check, monitor = VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION. CODE BLUE called; crash cart/defibrillator to bedside; FULL CODE verified (no DNR). BLS: started HIGH-QUALITY CPR immediately — rate 100-120/min, depth ≥2 in (≤2.4 in), FULL recoil, interruptions <10 s, ROTATE compressors q2min, firm surface, avoid hyperventilation; 30:2 until advanced airway then 1 breath q6s; pads applied + rhythm analyzed; waveform CAPNOGRAPHY (ETCO2 gauges quality — <10 = push harder/switch; sudden rise = ROSC). RHYTHM-BASED ACLS: SHOCKABLE (VF/pVT) → DEFIBRILLATE immediately ('clear' team) → resume CPR x2 min before recheck (minimize peri-shock pause); NON-SHOCKABLE (asystole/PEA) → do NOT shock, CPR + epi + fix cause. MEDS: EPINEPHRINE 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min (after 2nd shock in shockable); AMIODARONE 300 then 150 mg (or lidocaine) for REFRACTORY VF/pVT. IV/IO access; advanced airway without long CPR pause. REVERSIBLE CAUSES (H's & T's): Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, H+/acidosis, Hypo-/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia; Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (PE/MI — likely coronary here) — POC K+/glucose/ABG + ultrasound. TEAM LEADERSHIP (high-performance): ONE clear LEADER keeps overview + assigns ROLES + monitors quality + decides (no tunnel-vision); roles = compressors (rotate), airway/RT, defib operator, MED nurse, RECORDER/timer (logs compressions/shocks/meds/rhythm + prompts cycles + epi intervals), runner, family liaison. CLOSED-LOOP communication: directed order to a NAMED person → repeat-back → confirmation. First nurse LEADS until code team arrives. DELEGATE by scope: UAP = compressions/equipment/runner; RN = assessment/defib/meds. ROSC = pulse + organized rhythm (ETCO2 rise) → POST-ARREST care: titrate O2 (avoid HYPEROXIA, SpO2 ~92-98%) + avoid hypocapnia; support BP (post-arrest hypotension 94/58 → fluids/pressors); 12-lead + emergent PCI for STEMI; TTM if comatose; treat cause; ICU handoff. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT delay CPR/defib for access/documentation, did NOT shock asystole/PEA, did NOT pause compressions excessively or hyperventilate, did NOT run a leaderless code, did NOT override a valid DNR. DEBRIEF held after the event.
providerOrders: EMERGENCY: in-hospital cardiac arrest (code blue) — witnessed, monitored; initial rhythm ventricular fibrillation; FULL CODE (no DNR)

BLS: start high-quality CPR immediately — rate 100-120/min, depth ≥2 in (≤2.4 in), full recoil, interruptions <10 s, rotate compressors every 2 minutes, firm surface; 30:2 until advanced airway, then continuous compressions with 1 breath every 6 seconds; avoid hyperventilation

DEFIBRILLATION: apply pads and analyze rhythm; defibrillate shockable rhythms (VF/pulseless VT) immediately and resume CPR for 2 minutes before the next rhythm/pulse check; 'clear' the team before each shock; do NOT defibrillate asystole/PEA
Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes (as soon as possible in non-shockable arrest; after the second shock in shockable arrest)
Amiodarone 300 mg IV/IO (repeat 150 mg) — or lidocaine — for VF/pulseless VT refractory to defibrillation

ACCESS/MONITORING: establish IV or IO access; consider advanced airway without prolonged interruption of compressions; attach waveform capnography (sudden ETCO2 rise = ROSC; ETCO2 <10 mmHg = improve compression quality)

REVERSIBLE CAUSES: identify and treat the H's and T's (hypovolemia, hypoxia, hydrogen ion/acidosis, hypo-/hyperkalemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis — pulmonary/coronary); point-of-care potassium/glucose/ABG; consider bedside ultrasound

TEAM/LEADERSHIP: designate a team leader who assigns roles (compressors, airway/RT, defibrillator, medication nurse, recorder/timekeeper, runner, family liaison) and uses closed-loop communication (directed order → repeat-back → confirmation); delegate within scope (UAP: compressions/equipment/runner; RN: assessment/defibrillation/medications)
CODE STATUS: verify full code; honor any valid DNR/POLST order

POST-ROSC: titrate oxygen to SpO2 ~92-98% (avoid hyperoxia) and avoid hypocapnia; support blood pressure (fluids/vasopressors); obtain 12-lead ECG and pursue emergent coronary angiography/PCI for STEMI; initiate targeted temperature management if comatose; identify/treat the cause; transfer to ICU with structured handoff
AFTER EVENT: conduct a team debrief; complete the code documentation

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at the moment of this witnessed arrest?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Unresponsive, not breathing (only agonal gasps), and pulseless within a ≤10-second check → cardiac arrest; call a code blue and get the crash cart/defibrillator
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — rapid arrest recognition and activation drive everything that follows. RN role: recognize arrest + call for help.

opts.text: The monitor shows ventricular fibrillation — a SHOCKABLE rhythm requiring early defibrillation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — VF is shockable; early defibrillation is the key survival determinant. RN role: identify the shockable rhythm.

opts.text: Start high-quality CPR immediately — rate 100-120/min, depth ≥2 in, full recoil, minimal interruptions, on a firm surface
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — high-quality CPR is the foundation of survival. RN role: begin high-quality compressions without delay.

opts.text: A clear team leader and assigned roles (compressions, airway, defibrillator, medications, recorder) with closed-loop communication are needed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a coordinated high-performance team is essential to an effective code. RN role: establish leadership and roles.

opts.text: Establish IV/IO access, prepare epinephrine and an antiarrhythmic, and attach waveform capnography
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — access, medications, and capnography support the algorithm and gauge CPR quality. RN role: access + medications + monitoring.

opts.text: Search for and treat reversible causes — the H's and T's — and verify the patient is a full code (no DNR)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — reversible causes must be corrected, and code status governs whether ACLS proceeds. RN role: address causes + confirm code status.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — note for medication administration
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant before code medications. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Delay chest compressions and defibrillation to first obtain IV access, draw labs, and complete a full assessment
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Compressions and early defibrillation are the time-critical priorities; access/labs/documentation must not delay them.

opts.text: Have everyone focus on compressions while no one takes a leadership/overview role or assigns tasks
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A leaderless, unassigned code is chaotic and error-prone; one leader must maintain the overview and assign roles.

opts.text: VF and pulseless VT are shockable — immediate defibrillation is the key determinant of survival, and CPR is resumed for 2 minutes right after each shock
opts.rat: CORRECT — shockable-rhythm management. Foundation for ACLS.

opts.text: Asystole and PEA are non-shockable — do NOT defibrillate; treat with high-quality CPR, epinephrine, and correction of the cause
opts.rat: CORRECT — non-shockable-rhythm management (and the 'don't shock asystole' rule). Foundation for ACLS.

opts.text: Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO is given every 3-5 minutes, and an antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or lidocaine) is added for refractory VF/pVT
opts.rat: CORRECT — medication principles. Foundation for drug therapy in the code.

opts.text: High-quality CPR means a rate of 100-120/min, depth ≥2 in (≤2.4 in), full recoil, interruptions under 10 seconds, compressor rotation every 2 minutes, and avoiding excessive ventilation
opts.rat: CORRECT — high-quality CPR metrics. Foundation for effective resuscitation.

opts.text: A high-performance team uses a defined leader, clear role assignment, and closed-loop communication (directed order → repeat-back → confirmation)
opts.rat: CORRECT — team-dynamics principle. Foundation for safe, coordinated codes.

opts.text: Reversible causes are the H's and T's (hypovolemia, hypoxia, hydrogen ion/acidosis, hypo-/hyperkalemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the reversible-cause checklist. Foundation for treating the underlying problem.

opts.text: Asystole should be treated with immediate defibrillation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Asystole is NON-shockable; defibrillation has no role — treat with CPR, epinephrine, and correcting the cause.

opts.text: Chest compressions should be paused frequently and for as long as needed for procedures such as IV placement and intubation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Interruptions must be minimized (<10 s); procedures are done with minimal/no interruption of compressions to preserve perfusion.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the immediate priorities for this VF arrest

opts.text: Start high-quality chest compressions immediately (while calling the code and getting the defibrillator)
opts.rat: #1 — Compressions begin at once and sustain perfusion until defibrillation. RN role: immediate high-quality CPR.

opts.text: Defibrillate the shockable VF rhythm as soon as the defibrillator is available, then immediately resume CPR
opts.rat: #2 — Early defibrillation is the key survival determinant for VF/pVT. RN role: defibrillate the shockable rhythm.

opts.text: Assign a team leader and roles and use closed-loop communication to organize the resuscitation
opts.rat: #3 — A coordinated team sustains quality and prevents errors throughout. RN role: leadership + role assignment.

opts.text: Establish IV/IO access and give epinephrine every 3-5 minutes (and an antiarrhythmic for refractory VF)
opts.rat: #4 — Medications support the algorithm once CPR/defibrillation and the team are underway. RN role: access + medications.

opts.text: Identify and treat reversible causes (H's and T's) and prepare for post-ROSC care
opts.rat: #5 — Correcting the cause and planning post-arrest care sustain the resuscitation and recovery. RN role: treat causes + transition to post-arrest care.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED during this code.

opts.text: Deliver high-quality compressions (100-120/min, ≥2 in, full recoil), rotating compressors every 2 minutes
opts.rat: Core high-quality CPR. RN role: effective compressions with rotation.

opts.text: Defibrillate the VF/pulseless VT, then immediately resume CPR for 2 minutes before the next rhythm check
opts.rat: Correct management of a shockable rhythm with minimal peri-shock pause. RN role: defibrillate + resume CPR.

opts.text: Establish IV/IO access and give epinephrine 1 mg every 3-5 minutes; give amiodarone for refractory VF
opts.rat: Correct ACLS medication therapy. RN role: access + scheduled medications.

opts.text: Assign a clear team leader and roles and use closed-loop communication for every order
opts.rat: High-performance team structure that prevents errors. RN role: leadership + closed-loop communication.

opts.text: Search for and treat reversible causes (H's and T's) and attach capnography to gauge CPR quality and detect ROSC
opts.rat: Treating the cause and monitoring quality/ROSC. RN role: address causes + capnography.

opts.text: Delegate within scope — UAP perform compressions and retrieve equipment while the RN gives medications and defibrillates
opts.rat: Appropriate delegation by scope during a code. RN role: delegate compressions/equipment, retain licensed tasks.

opts.text: Defibrillate the rhythm even when it is asystole or PEA
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — asystole/PEA are non-shockable; defibrillation has no role and wastes time. Treat with CPR, epinephrine, and correcting the cause.

opts.text: Pause compressions for prolonged periods for IV placement, intubation, and documentation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — long interruptions reduce perfusion and survival; minimize pauses (<10 s) and perform procedures around continuous compressions.

opts.text: Have the team leader personally do compressions, manage the airway, and push medications alone without delegating
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the leader must keep the overview and delegate; getting tunnel-visioned on tasks degrades the whole resuscitation.

opts.text: Hyperventilate the patient with rapid, high-volume breaths throughout the code
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — excessive ventilation raises intrathoracic pressure, reduces venous return/coronary perfusion, and worsens outcomes; ventilate at the recommended rate.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm unresponsiveness/pulselessness (≤10 s), call the code blue, and start high-quality CPR; call for the crash cart/defibrillator
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Recognition, activation, and immediate compressions start the chain of survival. RN role: recognize + call + start CPR.

opts.text: Attach the defibrillator, analyze the rhythm, and defibrillate the VF immediately; resume CPR right away
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Early defibrillation of the shockable rhythm is the key survival intervention. RN role: defibrillate + resume CPR.

opts.text: Assign the team leader and roles and establish closed-loop communication
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Organizing a high-performance team sustains quality and coordination for the rest of the code. RN role: leadership + roles.

opts.text: Establish IV/IO access and give epinephrine (and amiodarone for refractory VF) on schedule, minimizing CPR interruptions
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Scheduled medications support the algorithm while compressions continue. RN role: access + medications.

opts.text: Identify and treat reversible causes (H's and T's); on ROSC, begin post-cardiac-arrest care and hand off to ICU
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/after ROSC. Correcting the cause and transitioning to post-arrest care complete the resuscitation. RN role: treat causes + post-arrest care.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action during the code, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The team kept compression interruptions under 10 seconds and rotated compressors every 2 minutes to maintain quality
opts.rat: High-quality CPR with minimal interruptions and compressor rotation maximizes perfusion. Correct practice.

opts.text: VF was defibrillated promptly, and CPR was resumed immediately after the shock without a prolonged pulse check
opts.rat: Early defibrillation with immediate resumption of CPR minimizes the peri-shock pause. Correct practice.

opts.text: The leader used closed-loop communication: 'Sara, give 1 mg epinephrine IV now' → 'Giving 1 mg epinephrine IV now' → 'Epinephrine given'
opts.rat: Directed order, repeat-back, and confirmation prevent medication/communication errors. Correct practice.

opts.text: Epinephrine was given every 3-5 minutes and amiodarone was added for refractory VF, with capnography monitored throughout
opts.rat: Correct medication timing for shockable arrest plus quality/ROSC monitoring. Correct practice.

opts.text: On ROSC, the team titrated oxygen (avoiding hyperoxia), supported blood pressure, obtained a 12-lead ECG, and initiated post-arrest care/TTM with handoff
opts.rat: Organized post-cardiac-arrest care optimizes neurologic and survival outcomes. Correct practice.

opts.text: When a rhythm check showed asystole, the team delivered a defibrillation shock
opts.rat: ⚠️ Asystole is NON-shockable; defibrillation has no role and interrupts CPR. Treat with CPR, epinephrine, and correcting the cause.

opts.text: Compressions were repeatedly paused for long stretches for IV access and documentation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Prolonged interruptions reduce perfusion and survival; minimize pauses and work around continuous compressions.

opts.text: No one took a leadership/overview role; orders were shouted to no one in particular and were never confirmed
opts.rat: ⚠️ A leaderless code with un-acknowledged orders causes errors and duplication; one leader must direct and use closed-loop communication.

opts.text: The patient was hyperventilated with rapid, forceful breaths for the duration of the code
opts.rat: ⚠️ Excessive ventilation raises intrathoracic pressure and reduces venous return/coronary perfusion, worsening outcomes. Ventilate at the recommended rate.

opts.text: The team ran full ACLS without ever checking code status, despite a valid DNR order documented on the chart
opts.rat: ⚠️ A valid DNR must be honored; performing resuscitation against it violates the patient's documented wishes. Always verify code status.
